question: Lithium has two stable isotopes with very different quantum statistics: $^6$Li (fermion, nuclear spin $I=1$) and $^7$Li (boson, $I=3/2$). The D2 cooling transition for both is:

options: $2S_{1/2} \to 2P_{3/2}$ at 671 nm; $\Gamma/2\pi = 5.87$ MHz; excited hyperfine unresolved because $\Delta E_{\rm hfs}' \sim \Gamma$
D$_2$ line at 780 nm for both Li isotopes, identical to rubidium (mass effect negligible)
$^6$Li and $^7$Li have measurably different D$_2$ wavelengths due to the substantial isotope mass shift
Li lacks a D$_2$ line entirely because of its small nuclear spin and absence of $P_{3/2}$ level
passive.explanation: Li's excited $2P_{3/2}$ state has unresolved hyperfine structure (splitting $\sim 2\Gamma$), making standard MOT and sub-Doppler techniques less efficient than for Rb. The Doppler limit is $T_D = \hbar\Gamma/2k_B \approx 140\,\mu$K. Single-photon recoil velocity: $v_r = \hbar k/m \approx 9.9$ cm/s — the largest of the alkalis, leading to significant recoil heating. $^6$Li is the primary platform for studying degenerate Fermi gases and the BCS-BEC crossover.

active.prompt: Match each Li isotope to its quantum properties:

pairs.definition: Boson ($I = 3/2$); $F = 1$ and $F = 2$ ground states; BEC achieved 1995 (Rice); attractive interactions at low field ($a < 0$)
Fermion ($I = 1$); fermionic quantum degeneracy; primary species for BCS-BEC crossover experiments
~300 G wide resonance near 832 G in the lowest two hyperfine states — allows precise tuning across BEC-BCS crossover
$v_r \approx 10$ cm/s (vs $\sim 6$ mm/s for Rb) — significant recoil heating limits sub-Doppler performance

active.explanation: $^7$Li was one of the first BECs (Rice, 1995), notable for having negative scattering length at zero field (attractive interactions) — the condensate is stabilised below a critical number $N_c \approx 1250$. The eventual collapse ('bosenova') was observed spectacularly in 2001 (JILA, $^{85}$Rb). $^6$Li remains the premier Fermi gas platform.

question: The broad Feshbach resonance in $^6$Li near $B = 832$ G allows tuning the s-wave scattering length from $a \to +\infty$ (BEC side) through unitarity to $a \to -\infty$ (BCS side). At the resonance centre:

options: Unitarity: $|a| \to \infty$, $\sigma$ saturated at $4\pi/k^2$; gas maximally interacting; properties universal in $n$ and $T$
At the Feshbach resonance the gas becomes non-interacting ($a = 0$) — the resonance is a zero-crossing of $a$
BCS gap $\Delta$ diverges at the centre of the resonance because the dimer binding energy goes to infinity
At unitarity the scattering cross-section is saturated at $\sigma = 8\pi/k^2$ (twice the s-wave unitarity limit)
passive.explanation: At unitarity, the scattering cross-section reaches the quantum limit $\sigma = 4\pi/k^2$ (unitarity limit) — the gas is as strongly interacting as quantum mechanics allows. All thermodynamic quantities are universal: $E/E_F = (3/5)\xi E_F$ where $\xi \approx 0.376$ (Bertsch parameter from QMC). The broad Li resonance ($\Delta B \approx 300$ G) allows precise field positioning to within 1 mG on the crossover. $^6$Li is the system where fermionic superfluidity was first confirmed (vortex lattices observed in 2005, MIT).

question: In $^6$Li, the three lowest hyperfine states $|1\rangle$, $|2\rangle$, $|3\rangle$ (at high field: $m_J = -1/2$, $m_I = +1, 0, -1$) exhibit SU(3) symmetry near Feshbach resonances. Three independently tunable interactions $a_{12}, a_{13}, a_{23}$ allow:

options: Realisation of SU(3) symmetric matter and Efimov physics with three distinguishable components — analogues of three-colour quarks in QCD can be simulated
SU(3) symmetry is broken by the hyperfine structure and cannot be restored by magnetic fields
The three states always have equal scattering lengths at any magnetic field
Three-component fermions cannot undergo pairing because Pauli exclusion forbids it
passive.explanation: At certain magnetic fields, $a_{12} \approx a_{13} \approx a_{23}$, giving approximate SU(3) symmetry. This system maps to a 3-component (3-colour) Fermi gas — a table-top quantum simulation of QCD-like physics. Efimov states involving three different components have been observed. Three-component Fermi gases may exhibit exotic phases: colour superfluidity, trion formation ($^6$Li bound state of all three), and phases analogous to colour-superconducting quark matter.
